lessons.title: Total Station Components and Setup
learning_objectives: Identify the principal components of a total station and explain the function of each.
Set up and orient a total station correctly over a point using centering and leveling.
Measure and apply the height of instrument (HI) and height of reflector (HR) for elevation work.
key_takeaways: A total station integrates an electronic theodolite (angles), an EDM (distances), and an onboard processor/data collector in one instrument.
A correct setup means the instrument is centered over the mark and the vertical axis is truly vertical (leveled); both must be true simultaneously.
Height of instrument (HI) is measured to the trunnion axis and height of reflector (HR) to the prism center; both are required for trigonometric elevations.

content.html:
What a Total Station Is

A total station is a single instrument that combines an electronic theodolite (for measuring horizontal and vertical/zenith angles), an electronic distance meter (EDM) (for measuring slope distance to a reflector or a surface), and an onboard microprocessor that combines those raw observations into coordinates in real time. It typically writes results to an internal memory or an external data collector. Because one instrument captures angle and distance to a point from one setup, a surveyor can locate features and corners by radial observation rather than the chained-distance-and-turned-angle routine of older theodolite-and-tape work.

The major external parts are the telescope (with crosshairs/reticle), the standards carrying the horizontal (trunnion) axis, the horizontal and vertical circles read electronically, the tribrach with leveling foot screws and an optical or laser plummet, and the keyboard/display. Internally, electronic angle encoders and the EDM emitter/receiver do the measuring. Understanding which part defines which axis is the key to understanding setup, calibration, and error.

rows: Sights the target; line of sight defines the collimation axis
Measures slope distance to a prism or surface by phase/pulse timing
Detachable base; foot screws level the instrument
Centers the vertical axis over the ground mark

content.html:
Centering and Leveling

A correct setup satisfies two independent conditions at once: the instrument's vertical axis must pass through the ground mark (centering) and that vertical axis must be truly plumb (leveling). A practical sequence is: spread the tripod at a comfortable height with the head roughly level and roughly over the point; press the legs in; use the optical/laser plummet to bring the instrument over the mark by adjusting two legs; use the foot screws to center the circular bubble; then loosen the tribrach clamp and slide the instrument to recenter the plummet exactly; finally use the plate (tubular) level and foot screws to level precisely, checking the bubble stays centered as you rotate the instrument 90° and 180°.

The two operations interact: centering with the plummet is only valid after the instrument is level, because tilting the vertical axis moves the plummet line off the mark. Iterate — level, recenter, re-level — until both hold simultaneously. A setup that is centered but not level, or level but not centered, injects systematic position error into every shot taken from it.

content.text: Centering error is unforgiving on short sights: an instrument or target 5 mm off the mark produces a far larger angular error on a 10 m shot than on a 200 m shot. On short setups (building corners, control ties), force-centering with tribrach adapters and careful plummet checks matters most.

content.html:
Height of Instrument and Height of Reflector

To get elevations from a total station you must record two vertical offsets. The height of instrument (HI) is the vertical distance from the occupied point (the mark on the ground) up to the instrument's trunnion (horizontal) axis — the tilting axis of the telescope, not the top of the tribrach. The height of reflector (HR), also called target height or rod height, is the vertical distance from the observed point up to the center of the prism. Both are typed into the data collector before observing.

The instrument measures the slope distance and the zenith angle to the prism; from these it computes the vertical component between the trunnion axis and the prism center. The point's elevation is then the occupied elevation plus HI plus that vertical component minus HR. A single wrong HI or HR offsets every elevation from that setup by the same amount — a classic blunder. Always measure HI to the marked index on the side of the instrument, confirm the prism pole height, and note both in the field record.

content.label: Trigonometric elevation from a total station setup (Z = zenith angle, S = slope distance)

content.alt: Total station set up over a point showing HI to the trunnion axis and a prism pole showing HR to the prism center
content.caption: Figure: Vertical geometry of a setup — HI to the trunnion axis, HR to the prism center, and the measured slope distance and zenith angle between them.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: A total station = electronic theodolite (angles) + EDM (distances) + processor/data collector in one unit.
Setup requires simultaneous centering (axis over the mark) and leveling (axis plumb); the two interact and must be iterated.
Measure HI to the trunnion axis and HR to the prism center; a wrong HI/HR offsets every elevation equally.
Centering error hurts most on short sights, so use force-centering and careful plummet checks on close shots.

flashcards.definition: An instrument integrating an electronic theodolite, an EDM, and an onboard processor/data collector, so that angle and distance to a point are captured from a single setup and combined into coordinates.
The vertical distance from the occupied ground mark up to the instrument's trunnion (horizontal) axis. Required, together with HR, to compute trigonometric elevations.
flashcards.hint_1: Measured to the tilting axis of the telescope, not the top of the tribrach.

flashcards.definition: Centering places the instrument's vertical axis directly over the ground mark; leveling makes that vertical axis truly plumb. Both must be true at once, so the surveyor iterates between the plummet and the foot screws.

lessons.title: Electronic Angle and Distance Measurement
learning_objectives: Explain how electronic angle encoders measure horizontal and vertical angles.
Describe how an EDM measures distance using phase comparison or pulse time-of-flight.
Reduce a slope distance to a horizontal distance using the zenith angle.
key_takeaways: Electronic angle encoders read the position of a graduated circle electronically, eliminating manual micrometer reading and reducing reading blunders.
An EDM measures slope distance by comparing the phase of returned modulated light (phase method) or timing a pulse's round trip (pulse method).
Horizontal distance is recovered from slope distance with H = S·sin(Z), where Z is the zenith angle, after applying atmospheric and instrument corrections.

content.html:
Electronic Angle Measurement

A total station replaces the optical micrometer of a classical theodolite with an electronic angle encoder on each circle. Two common designs exist. Absolute encoders carry a coded pattern (often a binary or pseudo-random track) so that each position on the circle has a unique, directly readable code; the instrument knows its orientation the moment it powers up. Incremental encoders count fine, evenly spaced graduations as the circle turns, referenced to an index, and accumulate the count to a reading. In both, a light source and photodetector array sense the graduations, and the processor interpolates between lines to reach a resolution of a few arc-seconds.

Electronic reading removes the human micrometer-estimation error and the transposition blunders of hand-recorded directions, but it does not remove instrument-axis errors. Collimation (line of sight not perpendicular to the trunnion axis) and vertical-circle index error remain; they are removed by observing in both face left and face right (direct and reverse) and meaning the two, exactly as with an optical theodolite. Many total stations measure and store a dual-axis tilt-sensor correction to compensate small residual mislevelment of the vertical axis.

How an EDM Measures Distance

The electronic distance meter (EDM) sends modulated infrared or laser light to a retroreflective prism (or, in reflectorless mode, to a surface) and analyzes the returned signal. Two measuring principles dominate. In the phase-comparison method, the carrier is amplitude-modulated at several known wavelengths; the instrument measures the phase shift between outgoing and returning signals for each wavelength and resolves the integer number of whole wavelengths (the ‘ambiguity’) from the coarser modulations. In the pulse (time-of-flight) method, the instrument emits short light pulses and measures the elapsed travel time directly; distance is half the round-trip time multiplied by the speed of light in air.

Both methods depend on the velocity of light through the atmosphere, which varies with temperature, pressure, and humidity. The instrument applies a ppm (parts-per-million) atmospheric correction based on entered or sensed met data, plus a prism constant (the offset between the prism's optical center and the point it represents). Without the correct prism constant and ppm, distances carry a systematic scale/offset error. The result of the EDM is always a slope distance along the line of sight, which must still be reduced to horizontal and vertical components.

content.label: Reduction of slope distance S to horizontal (H) and vertical (V) using the zenith angle Z

content.html:
From Slope to Horizontal and Vertical

Surveying coordinates are computed in a horizontal plane, so the measured slope distance S must be split into a horizontal distance H and a vertical distance V. Using the zenith angle Z (measured from the vertical, so a level sight is Z = 90°), the geometry is simply H = S·sin(Z) and V = S·cos(Z). Some instruments report the vertical angle (from the horizontal) instead; then H = S·cos(v) and V = S·sin(v). The processor does this automatically, but the surveyor must know which convention is in use to check field values by hand.

For long, precise lines the instrument also applies earth-curvature and refraction corrections to the vertical component, because a level line of sight is not a true horizontal over distance and light bends in the atmosphere. The combined correction is commonly taken as about 0.0675 K² feet (K in thousands of feet) and improves trigonometric elevations on lines beyond a few hundred feet. For ordinary topo at short range the effect is negligible, but for control and long stakeout it is not.

content.text: Always confirm the prism constant in the data collector matches the prism actually on the pole (common values are 0 mm and −30 mm). Mixing a −30 mm prism with a 0 mm setting biases every distance by 30 mm — a silent, repeatable error that closure checks may not flag.

content.alt: Diagram of slope distance S, zenith angle Z, and the resolved horizontal H and vertical V components
content.caption: Figure: A slope distance S at zenith angle Z resolves into horizontal H = S·sin(Z) and vertical V = S·cos(Z).

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Electronic encoders (absolute or incremental) read angles directly, removing reading blunders but not collimation/index errors — still observe face left and face right.
EDMs measure slope distance by phase comparison or pulse time-of-flight; both need the correct prism constant and atmospheric (ppm) correction.
Reduce slope to horizontal with H = S·sin(Z) and to vertical with V = S·cos(Z) using the zenith angle.
Apply curvature-and-refraction correction to vertical components on long lines.

flashcards.definition: The distance-measuring component of a total station. It sends modulated light to a prism or surface and measures distance by phase comparison of the returned signal or by timing a pulse's round trip, then applies prism-constant and atmospheric corrections.
A vertical angle measured downward from the local vertical (zenith), so a perfectly level line of sight reads 90°. Horizontal distance = S·sin(Z); vertical distance = S·cos(Z).

The fixed offset between a retroreflective prism's optical center and the physical point it represents (commonly 0 mm or −30 mm). It must be entered correctly or every EDM distance carries that systematic error.
flashcards.hint_1: A mismatch produces a constant, repeatable bias in distance.

lessons.title: Data Collectors and Field Software
learning_objectives: Describe the role of a data collector and field software in a total-station workflow.
Explain how a job is set up: occupy, backsight, orientation, and point coding.
Distinguish raw observation data from reduced coordinate data and explain why both are kept.
key_takeaways: The data collector runs field software that controls the instrument, stores observations, and computes coordinates live, replacing the paper field book.
Every setup is oriented by occupying a known point and backsighting another known point (or a known azimuth) before any sideshots are taken.
Field software stores both reduced coordinates and the underlying raw data (angles, distances, HI/HR) so the work can be re-reduced and audited.

content.html:
The Data Collector's Role

A data collector is a rugged handheld (or the instrument's own onboard keypad) running field software that orchestrates the survey. It commands the total station to read angles and distances, prompts the surveyor for setup information, computes coordinates in real time, applies feature codes, and stores everything to a job file. It is the modern replacement for the paper field book: faster, less error-prone for arithmetic, and directly importable to office software. Common field packages include Trimble Access, Carlson SurvCE/SurvPC, Leica Captivate, and TopCon MAGNET Field, but the workflow concepts are the same across brands.

The collector manages a point database of known control, observed sideshots, and computed stakeout points, each with a number/name, coordinates, and a feature code. It also keeps the raw measurement record. Good field software lets the surveyor review a setup's residuals on the spot, re-shoot a bad backsight, and flag suspect points before leaving the site — catching errors while they are still cheap to fix.

Setting Up a Job: Occupy, Backsight, Orient

Before any feature is located, the setup must be oriented into the job's coordinate system. The standard sequence is: select or enter the occupied point (the known coordinate the instrument stands over) and its HI; select the backsight — either a second known point or a known azimuth/bearing — and its HR; sight the backsight precisely and accept it. The software then computes the orientation (the rotation that aligns the instrument's horizontal circle with grid north) so that every subsequent shot yields a correct coordinate. Many crews take a backsight check to a second known point to confirm the setup before collecting data.

From an oriented setup, each observed point (a sideshot or radial shot) is located by one angle-and-distance reading. The surveyor assigns a feature code (e.g., EP for edge of pavement, TREE, IP for iron pin) and optionally line/connect codes so the office software can draw linework automatically. Disciplined, consistent coding in the field is what turns a cloud of points into a usable drawing without hours of office cleanup.

rows: Where the instrument stands and its vertical offset
Reference direction to orient the horizontal circle
Rotation aligning the circle with the job's north
Independent confirmation the setup is correct
Attribute and connectivity for automated linework

content.html:
Raw Data vs. Reduced Coordinates

Field software stores two related but distinct things. Reduced coordinates are the final northings, eastings, and elevations the instrument computed for each point. Raw data are the underlying observations — occupied and backsight points, HI and HR, horizontal and zenith angles, slope distances, and prism/atmospheric settings — usually in a vendor format or the open .rw5 raw file. Coordinates are convenient, but raw data are the legal and technical record: they let the office re-reduce the survey if a control coordinate, HI, or prism constant was wrong, and they document exactly how each point was measured.

This separation matters for quality and defensibility. If a backsight is later found to be on the wrong point, a coordinate-only file is ruined, but a raw file can be reprocessed with the corrected backsight and every dependent point recomputed. For this reason, professional practice and survey standards expect the raw observations to be preserved, not just the tidy coordinate list.

content.text: Texas differs / practice note: Texas surveying standards (22 TAC Chapter 138, Subchapter E) require that the basis of bearing, monumentation, and the relationship between record monuments and surveyed boundaries be documented. Retaining the raw observation file (with HI/HR, backsight, and basis of bearing) is the practical way to support those records and to re-reduce work if control is later corrected.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: The data collector + field software controls the instrument, stores points and raw data, and computes coordinates in the field.
Orient every setup by occupying a known point and backsighting a known point/azimuth; verify with a backsight check.
Code features consistently in the field so office software can build linework automatically.
Always preserve the raw observation file, not just coordinates, so the survey can be audited and re-reduced.

flashcards.definition: A rugged handheld (or onboard keypad) running field software that controls the total station, stores points, raw observations, and feature codes, and computes coordinates in real time — the digital replacement for the paper field book.
A second known point (or a known azimuth) sighted from the occupied station to orient the instrument's horizontal circle to the job's coordinate system before any sideshots are taken.

The recorded underlying observations — occupied/backsight points, HI/HR, angles, slope distances, and instrument settings — as opposed to reduced coordinates. Raw data allow a survey to be re-reduced and audited if a control value was wrong.
flashcards.hint_1: Keep this, not just the coordinate list.

lessons.title: Stakeout Procedures
learning_objectives: Explain the stakeout (layout) workflow: loading design points and guiding to position.
Interpret the data collector's go/come, left/right, and cut/fill guidance.
Compute the bearing/azimuth and distance from a setup to a design point (inverse).
key_takeaways: Stakeout reverses topo: instead of recording where a point is, you guide a rod to where a design point should be.
The data collector computes the inverse from the instrument to each design point and directs the rodperson in/out and left/right, then reports cut or fill.
An oriented, checked setup and a verified design point list are prerequisites; a bad backsight stakes everything in the wrong place.

content.html:
Stakeout Is Topo in Reverse

Stakeout (also called layout or setting out) is the inverse of topographic data collection. In topo you observe an existing feature and the software records its coordinate. In stakeout you start with a design coordinate — a building corner, a curb return, a pipe invert, a property corner to set — and the instrument guides you to put a stake exactly there on the ground. Construction layout, boundary monument setting, and grade staking are all stakeout. The math is a coordinate inverse: given the occupied point and the design point, compute the azimuth and horizontal distance between them.

The crew first loads or enters the design point list (often from the engineer's coordinate file) into the data collector, sets up and orients over known control exactly as for topo, and runs a backsight check. Then for each design point the software directs the instrument operator and rodperson toward the target, reads the rod's current position, and reports how far and which way to move until the rod is within tolerance of the design coordinate.

Reading the Guidance

Modern field software gives intuitive directions. In/out (sometimes ‘go/come’ or ‘toward/away’) moves the rod along the line of sight from the instrument; left/right moves it perpendicular to that line, from the operator's or the rodperson's stated perspective. The collector updates these offsets each time a shot is taken to the prism, converging on the design point. When horizontal position is within tolerance, the software reports the vertical difference as cut (the ground is above design, dig down) or fill (the ground is below design, build up), computed from the staked elevation against the design elevation.

Robotic total stations let one person stake alone: the instrument tracks and locks onto the prism while the rodperson reads the guidance on a controller at the rod. Whether conventional or robotic, the rodperson should plumb the rod carefully and re-observe after driving the stake, because the act of setting the hub can nudge the position. Always record the as-staked coordinate so the set point is documented, not just intended.

content.label: Inverse: azimuth and horizontal distance from setup (N1,E1) to design point (N2,E2)

content.text: Garbage in, garbage out: stakeout faithfully reproduces whatever the orientation and design file say. A wrong backsight, a transposed northing/easting, or a design point on the wrong datum will be staked precisely — in the wrong place. Always run an independent check (a known point or a second control backsight) before setting production stakes.

rows: Move the rod toward or away from the instrument along the sight line
Move the rod perpendicular to the sight line
Ground is above design elevation — excavate down by that amount
Ground is below design elevation — build up by that amount
Horizontal offset is small enough to set the stake

content.alt: Plan view of a setup, a prism rod, and a target design point with in/out and left/right offsets labeled
content.caption: Figure: Stakeout guidance — the software resolves the rod-to-design vector into in/out (along sight) and left/right (perpendicular) corrections.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Stakeout starts from design coordinates and guides a stake to that ground position — the reverse of topo.
The collector inverses from the setup to each design point and reports in/out, left/right, then cut/fill.
A verified orientation and design list are mandatory; an error there is staked perfectly in the wrong place.
Re-observe and record the as-staked coordinate after driving the hub.

flashcards.definition: The process of placing stakes/monuments at predetermined design coordinates on the ground. It is the inverse of topo: the instrument guides a rod to where a point should be, rather than recording where an existing point is.

The computation of the azimuth and horizontal distance between two known coordinates, Az = atan2(ΔE, ΔN) and D = √(ΔN² + ΔE²). Stakeout software inverses from the setup to each design point to direct the rod.

In grade staking, cut means the existing ground is above the design elevation (excavate down) and fill means it is below (build up). The data collector reports the value once the horizontal position is within tolerance.
flashcards.hint_1: Cut = dig down; fill = build up.

lessons.title: Reflectorless Measurement
learning_objectives: Explain how reflectorless (direct-reflex) EDM measures distance to a surface without a prism.
Identify appropriate uses and the limitations/error sources of reflectorless measurement.
Apply safe and accurate practices for reflectorless shots on faces and edges.
key_takeaways: Reflectorless (direct-reflex) EDM measures to a surface using the light scattered back from the material, so no prism is needed.
It is ideal for inaccessible or unsafe points — building faces, cliff edges, traffic areas — but is less precise and range-limited compared with prism work.
Beam divergence, oblique surfaces, edges, and dark/wet materials degrade reflectorless accuracy; aim at the true point and verify critical shots.

content.html:
Measuring Without a Prism

Reflectorless measurement (also direct reflex, DR) lets a total station measure distance to a natural surface — brick, concrete, rock, asphalt — without a retroreflective prism. The EDM emits a narrow, often visible laser; the surface scatters a small fraction of that light back, and the receiver times or phase-compares it just as it would a prism return. Because diffuse surfaces return far less signal than a prism, reflectorless mode has a shorter range and lower precision, but it unlocks points a rodperson could never safely reach.

Typical reflectorless range runs from tens of meters to several hundred meters depending on the instrument, the surface reflectivity, and the angle of incidence. A light-colored, flat surface faced squarely returns the strongest signal; a dark, wet, glassy, or steeply angled surface returns much less and may yield a noisy or failed measurement. The prism constant must be set to the reflectorless (DR) value, not a prism offset, or the distance will be biased.

Where Reflectorless Shines — and Where It Doesn't

Reflectorless is invaluable for points that are inaccessible or unsafe: building corners high on a facade, bridge members, the face of a retaining wall, rock faces, the centerline of a busy roadway, or features inside a fenced enclosure. It speeds topographic and as-built work by letting one operator locate features without sending a rodperson into hazardous or restricted ground. For deformation monitoring of structures it can sample many points on a face quickly.

Its weaknesses follow from the physics. The laser beam has a finite footprint (beam divergence) that grows with distance; at long range the measured distance is an average over that spot, not a pinpoint. Aiming at an edge (a wall corner against the sky, a curb lip) can split the beam between near and far surfaces, returning a blended or wrong distance. Oblique incidence lengthens the effective path and weakens the return. Critical reflectorless points should be checked — shot twice, or compared with a prism shot to an adjacent reachable point.

content.text: Laser safety: the visible/invisible EDM beam in reflectorless mode is more powerful than a typical pointer. Never aim it at a person's eyes or at mirrored/reflective surfaces (glass, polished metal, vehicle mirrors), and follow the instrument's laser-class precautions. Reflective surfaces can also return spurious distances.

rows: Light/flat surfaces return strong signal; dark/wet/glassy surfaces weaken or fail it
Oblique surfaces weaken the return and bias the path
Spot grows with distance; reading averages over the footprint
Beam straddles near/far surfaces — blended, unreliable distance
Must be set to the DR value, not a prism offset

content.alt: Laser beam footprint growing with distance and straddling a building edge, illustrating reflectorless error sources
content.caption: Figure: Beam divergence enlarges the laser footprint with range; aiming near an edge can split the beam between near and far surfaces.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Reflectorless (DR) EDM measures to a surface via scattered light — no prism, but shorter range and lower precision.
It is ideal for inaccessible/unsafe points; set the prism constant to the DR value, not a prism offset.
Beam divergence, edges, oblique angles, and dark/wet/glassy surfaces degrade accuracy — aim at the true point and verify critical shots.
Observe laser-safety precautions; never aim at eyes or reflective surfaces.

flashcards.definition: EDM measurement to a natural surface without a prism, using the light scattered back from the material. It reaches inaccessible points but has shorter range and lower precision than prism measurement.
The widening of the EDM laser footprint with distance. At range the reflectorless reading averages over the spot, so it cannot resolve a feature finer than the footprint and can straddle edges.
flashcards.hint_1: Why aiming near a wall corner can give a blended distance.

lessons.title: Field-to-Office Data Transfer
learning_objectives: Distinguish coordinate files from raw data files and describe common formats.
Explain the field-to-office workflow: download, import, re-reduce, and draft.
Apply data-management practices that preserve a defensible record of the survey.
key_takeaways: Two file families move from field to office: coordinate files (point lists) and raw data files (the observations).
Raw files (e.g., .rw5) let the office re-reduce the survey, while coordinate files (e.g., comma-delimited PNEZD) feed the drawing/COGO directly.
Reliable transfer, consistent point/feature coding, and archived raw data are what make the work efficient and defensible.

content.html:
Getting Data Out of the Field

Once observations are collected, the job must move from the data collector to the office software for adjustment, COGO, and drafting. Transfer happens by USB cable, removable card, Wi-Fi/Bluetooth, or cloud sync, depending on the system. The office software — Carlson, Civil 3D, TBC (Trimble Business Center), MicroSurvey, etc. — imports the field files, recomputes or adjusts as needed, and turns coded points into linework and a finished survey drawing. The goal is a clean, lossless handoff: nothing renamed, nothing dropped, and the raw observations preserved.

Two distinct file families travel together. A coordinate file is a simple list of points and their reduced coordinates, often comma-delimited in a fixed order such as PNEZD (Point, Northing, Easting, Z/elevation, Description). A raw data file contains the underlying observations — setups, backsights, HI/HR, angles, distances — in a vendor or open format. The coordinate file is what most quickly populates a drawing; the raw file is what protects you when something needs to be reprocessed.

rows: ASCII PNEZD (.csv/.txt): point, N, E, Z, desc
Importing points and linework into CAD/COGO
Sharing points, surfaces, alignments between packages

content.html:
The Office Workflow

A typical sequence in the office is: (1) download the job (raw + coordinate files) and back it up immediately; (2) import the raw file into the office software and re-reduce it — confirming control coordinates, HI/HR, prism constants, and basis of bearing — correcting any field setup error and recomputing every dependent point; (3) adjust any traverse or network for closure; (4) import or generate the coordinate/point file with feature codes; and (5) draft the survey, building linework from the codes and adding annotation. Re-reducing from raw, rather than trusting the field coordinates blindly, is what lets you fix a mis-keyed backsight or HI after the fact.

Throughout, data management discipline matters: keep a consistent point-numbering scheme so two jobs do not collide, maintain a shared feature-code library so EP/TREE/IP mean the same thing every time, and archive the raw files with the project. The transfer is also where units and datum mismatches surface — confirm U.S. survey foot vs. international foot, and the correct State Plane zone/datum, before merging into existing control.

content.text: Texas note: State Plane work in Texas uses NAD83 in one of several Texas zones, and the U.S. survey foot has historically been the convention. Mixing the international foot with U.S.-survey-foot State Plane coordinates introduces a scale error that grows with distance from the origin — confirm the foot definition and zone on import.

PNEZD order matters: many imports fail or silently swap Northing and Easting if the column order in the file does not match what the software expects. Always confirm the field order (PNEZD vs. PENZD) when importing an ASCII coordinate file.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Coordinate files (e.g., PNEZD ASCII) carry reduced points; raw files (e.g., .rw5) carry the observations.
Office workflow: download/back up → import and re-reduce raw → adjust → generate coordinates/codes → draft.
Re-reducing from raw lets you correct a field setup error and recompute dependent points.
Manage point numbering and feature codes consistently, archive raw data, and verify foot definition and State Plane zone/datum on import.

flashcards.definition: An ASCII point list, commonly comma-delimited in Point–Northing–Easting–Z(elevation)–Description order, used to import reduced points and linework into CAD/COGO software.
An open XML exchange format for transferring survey data — points, surfaces, alignments, parcels — between different software packages without proprietary lock-in.

Recomputing a survey in the office from its raw observations — verifying control, HI/HR, prism constants, and basis of bearing — so that a field setup error can be corrected and all dependent points recomputed.

lessons.title: Total Station Calibration and Care
learning_objectives: Identify the instrument errors checked by calibration and how field procedures remove them.
Describe resection/free-stationing and radial topo as field methods enabled by total stations.
Apply routine care, field checks, and maintenance to keep a total station in tolerance.
key_takeaways: Collimation, trunnion-axis tilt, and vertical-index errors are checked by calibration; observing face left/face right cancels many of them in the field.
Resection (free-stationing) places the instrument anywhere and solves its coordinates by sighting two or more known points — no setup over a known mark required.
Radial topo locates many points by single angle-and-distance shots from one oriented setup; careful care and periodic calibration keep results trustworthy.

content.html:
Instrument Errors and Calibration

Even a well-built total station carries small mechanical and electronic axis errors that calibration measures and the firmware (or field procedure) compensates. The chief ones are line-of-sight (collimation) error — the sight line not perpendicular to the trunnion axis; trunnion (horizontal) axis tilt — that axis not perpendicular to the vertical axis; vertical-circle index error — the zenith reading not exactly 90° on a level sight; and EDM additive (zero) constant and scale errors. Manufacturers provide an onboard calibration routine that has the operator sight a target in both faces; the instrument computes and stores correction values it then applies to every reading.

Many of these errors are also self-cancelling in good field practice: observing each angle in face left and face right and meaning the two removes collimation, trunnion-tilt, and vertical-index error to first order. Calibration is what keeps single-face shots honest and flags an instrument that has drifted out of tolerance. EDM additive constant should be checked against a baseline of known distances periodically, especially after a knock or a repair.

rows: Sight line not ⊥ to trunnion axis
Horizontal axis not ⊥ to vertical axis

content.html:
Resection and Free-Stationing

A great advantage of the total station is that you need not occupy a known monument at all. In resection (also free-stationing), the instrument is set up at a convenient unknown point and sights two or more known control points; from the observed angles and distances the software solves the instrument's own coordinates and orientation. This frees the surveyor to set up wherever the visibility and geometry are best — in the middle of a site, off the actual control — and is the backbone of construction layout from a temporary ‘free station’.

Resection geometry matters: sighting points that are nearly in line with the instrument, or clustered in a narrow angle, gives a weak, ill-conditioned solution. Strong resections use well-distributed control with good intersection angles, and using three or more known points provides redundancy and a residual check on the fit. Distances (not just angles) to the known points greatly strengthen the solution, which is why a total-station resection is far more robust than the classical angle-only three-point problem.

content.latex: N_P = N_1 + D\cos(Az), \qquad E_P = E_1 + D\sin(Az)
content.label: Radial coordinate of a point P from an oriented setup at (N1,E1): azimuth Az, horizontal distance D

content.html:
Radial Topo and Routine Care

Radial topography (radial topo) is the everyday total-station method: from a single oriented setup, each feature is located by one horizontal direction and one slope distance, and the processor radiates a coordinate from the station to the point using N_P = N_1 + D·cos(Az) and E_P = E_1 + D·sin(Az). Hundreds of points can be picked up quickly this way; combined with resection, a crew can set up wherever convenient, orient, and radiate topo without ever standing on a monument.

None of this is trustworthy if the instrument is mistreated. Care practices: transport in its padded case; never leave it unattended on the tripod; carry it upright and lock the clamps lightly; keep the lens and reflectors clean with proper optics cloth; protect the EDM optics from rain and dust; let the instrument equalize to ambient temperature before precise work; and avoid sighting through heat shimmer. Field checks: a daily two-face check on a distant target, a periodic EDM baseline check, and a backsight check every setup catch problems before they corrupt a job. Send the instrument for professional calibration on the manufacturer's schedule or after any hard knock.

content.text: Texas / standards note: Texas surveying standards (22 TAC §138.83, Precision and Accuracy) set positional/closure tolerances for the survey category being performed. Keeping the instrument calibrated, performing two-face and backsight checks, and documenting them is how a surveyor demonstrates the measurements meet the required accuracy.

content.alt: Free-station setup sighting three known control points, with radial topo shots fanning out to located features
content.caption: Figure: A resected (free-station) setup solves its own coordinates from known points, then radiates topo shots to features by single angle-and-distance observations.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Collimation, trunnion-tilt, and vertical-index errors are removed by two-face observation and stored calibration corrections; check the EDM additive constant on a baseline.
Resection/free-stationing solves the instrument's coordinates from two or more known points — set up anywhere; use 3+ well-distributed points for a strong solution.
Radial topo radiates a coordinate to each feature from one oriented setup using N = N₁ + D·cos(Az), E = E₁ + D·sin(Az).
Routine care, daily two-face and backsight checks, temperature equalization, and scheduled calibration keep results within standard tolerances.

flashcards.definition: An instrument error in which the line of sight is not perpendicular to the trunnion (horizontal) axis. It is removed by meaning face-left and face-right observations and by stored calibration corrections.

A method that places the instrument at an unknown point and solves its own coordinates and orientation by observing two or more known control points. It lets the surveyor set up wherever visibility and geometry are best, without occupying a monument.
flashcards.hint_1: Use three or more well-distributed points for a strong, checked solution.

flashcards.definition: Locating features by single horizontal-direction and slope-distance observations from one oriented setup; the processor radiates a coordinate to each point as N = N₁ + D·cos(Az), E = E₁ + D·sin(Az).
Observing a target in both face left (direct) and face right (reverse) and meaning the readings, which cancels collimation, trunnion-axis tilt, and vertical-index errors to first order.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which three measuring functions does a total station integrate into one instrument?
options: GPS positioning, leveling, and photogrammetry
Electronic angle measurement, electronic distance measurement, and onboard processing/storage
Magnetic compass, chaining, and plane-table plotting
Barometric altimetry, tape distance, and manual angle reading
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Electronic angle measurement, electronic distance measurement, and onboard processing/storage
quiz_questions.explanation: A total station combines an electronic theodolite (angles), an EDM (distances), and a microprocessor/data collector. The other options describe unrelated or obsolete techniques.

quiz_questions.question_text: The height of instrument (HI) entered in the data collector should be measured to which part of the instrument?
options: The top of the tripod head
The center of the prism
The trunnion (horizontal) axis
The bottom of the tribrach
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The trunnion (horizontal) axis
quiz_questions.explanation: HI is the vertical distance from the occupied mark to the trunnion axis (the telescope's tilting axis), because that is where the vertical angle is measured from. HR is measured to the prism center.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why must centering and leveling be iterated rather than done once each?
options: The EDM cannot read until both are perfect
Tilting the vertical axis during leveling moves the plummet line off the mark, so re-centering is needed
The data collector overwrites the coordinate each time
Foot screws change the prism constant
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Tilting the vertical axis during leveling moves the plummet line off the mark, so re-centering is needed
quiz_questions.explanation: Centering is only valid when the instrument is level, and leveling can shift the plummet off the point. The two conditions interact, so the surveyor alternates between them until both hold simultaneously.

quiz_questions.question_text: Observing a horizontal angle in both face left and face right and meaning the two readings primarily removes which errors?
options: Atmospheric refraction and earth curvature
Collimation, trunnion-axis tilt, and vertical-index errors
Prism constant and ppm scale errors
Centering and HI measurement blunders
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Collimation, trunnion-axis tilt, and vertical-index errors
quiz_questions.explanation: Two-face observation cancels instrument-axis errors (collimation, trunnion tilt, vertical index) to first order. Atmospheric, prism-constant, and centering errors are addressed by other means.

quiz_questions.question_text: A slope distance of 250.00 ft is measured at a zenith angle of 86°00'. What is the horizontal distance (to 0.01 ft)?
Setting a 0 mm prism constant while actually using a −30 mm prism will cause:
options: A random scatter in the measured angles
A constant, repeatable 30 mm bias in every measured distance
An error only on reflectorless shots
No effect, because the software auto-detects the prism
quiz_questions.correct_answer: A constant, repeatable 30 mm bias in every measured distance
quiz_questions.explanation: The prism constant is a fixed offset between the prism's optical center and the point. A mismatch biases every distance by that amount — a systematic, repeatable error that closure checks may not reveal.

quiz_questions.question_text: An EDM that times how long a light pulse takes to travel to the target and back is using which measuring principle?
options: Phase comparison
Pulse time-of-flight
Triangulation
Barometric ranging
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Pulse time-of-flight
quiz_questions.explanation: The pulse (time-of-flight) method measures the round-trip travel time of a light pulse; distance is half that time times the speed of light in air. Phase comparison instead measures the phase shift of a continuously modulated signal.

quiz_questions.question_text: Before collecting any sideshots, an oriented total-station setup requires which two known references?
options: Two backsights and no occupied point
An occupied (known) point and a backsight to a known point or azimuth
A GPS fix and a benchmark
Two reflectorless surfaces
quiz_questions.correct_answer: An occupied (known) point and a backsight to a known point or azimuth
quiz_questions.explanation: Orientation needs the occupied point (where the instrument stands) and a backsight (a known point or known azimuth) so the software can align the horizontal circle to the job's coordinate system.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why do professionals preserve the raw observation file rather than only the reduced coordinate file?
options: Raw files are smaller and load faster
Coordinates cannot be drafted in CAD
Raw data allow the survey to be re-reduced and audited if a control value, HI, or backsight was wrong
The data collector deletes coordinates after upload
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Raw data allow the survey to be re-reduced and audited if a control value, HI, or backsight was wrong
quiz_questions.explanation: Raw observations (angles, distances, HI/HR, backsight) are the technical record. If a control coordinate or backsight is later found wrong, the survey can be reprocessed; a coordinate-only file cannot be corrected.

quiz_questions.question_text: Assigning consistent feature codes (e.g., EP, TREE, IP) in the field is important mainly because:
options: It increases EDM accuracy
It lets office software build linework automatically and reduces drafting cleanup
It is required to power on the instrument
It changes the coordinate system
quiz_questions.correct_answer: It lets office software build linework automatically and reduces drafting cleanup
quiz_questions.explanation: Feature and line/connect codes let the office software draw linework and attribute points automatically, turning a cloud of shots into a drawing without manual reconstruction.

quiz_questions.question_text: Stakeout (layout) differs from topographic data collection in that:
options: It records where existing features are located
It guides a stake to a predetermined design coordinate — the reverse of topo
It requires no instrument orientation
It cannot report elevations
quiz_questions.correct_answer: It guides a stake to a predetermined design coordinate — the reverse of topo
quiz_questions.explanation: Topo records where points are; stakeout starts from design coordinates and guides a stake to that ground position. Both still require a properly oriented, checked setup.

quiz_questions.question_text: During grade staking the collector reports 'CUT 0.4 ft' at a point. This means:
options: The ground is below design and must be filled 0.4 ft
The ground is above design elevation and must be excavated down 0.4 ft
The horizontal position is 0.4 ft off
The prism height is wrong by 0.4 ft
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The ground is above design elevation and must be excavated down 0.4 ft
quiz_questions.explanation: Cut means existing ground is above the design elevation, so 0.4 ft must be excavated. Fill would mean the ground is below design and material must be added.

quiz_questions.question_text: A free station at N 5000.00, E 5000.00 must stake a design point at N 5120.00, E 5050.00. What is the horizontal distance to stake (to 0.01 ft)?
Stakeout will faithfully set production stakes in the wrong location if the backsight orientation is wrong.
options: True
False
quiz_questions.correct_answer: True
quiz_questions.explanation: Stakeout reproduces whatever the orientation and design file say. A wrong backsight, datum, or transposed coordinate is staked precisely in the wrong place — hence the need for an independent check before production staking.

quiz_questions.question_text: Compared with prism measurement, reflectorless (direct-reflex) measurement generally has:
options: Longer range and higher precision
Shorter range and lower precision, but reaches inaccessible points
Identical performance in all conditions
No dependence on surface material
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Shorter range and lower precision, but reaches inaccessible points
quiz_questions.explanation: A diffuse surface returns far less signal than a prism, so reflectorless has shorter range and lower precision, but it can locate points no rodperson could safely reach.

quiz_questions.question_text: Aiming a reflectorless shot at the corner edge of a building against open sky can produce a wrong distance because:
options: The prism constant changes at edges
The laser footprint can straddle near and far surfaces, returning a blended distance
Edges absorb all the laser light
Zenith angles cannot be read near edges
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The laser footprint can straddle near and far surfaces, returning a blended distance
quiz_questions.explanation: Beam divergence gives the laser a finite footprint. Near an edge that spot can split between the near surface and whatever is behind it, yielding a blended or wrong distance — so critical points should be verified.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which surface condition gives the strongest, most reliable reflectorless return?
options: A dark, wet, glassy surface at a steep oblique angle
A light-colored flat surface faced squarely (near-perpendicular incidence)
A mirrored or polished metal panel
An edge silhouetted against the sky
quiz_questions.correct_answer: A light-colored flat surface faced squarely (near-perpendicular incidence)
quiz_questions.explanation: Strong returns come from light, flat surfaces at near-normal incidence. Dark/wet/glassy or oblique surfaces weaken the return, and mirrored surfaces can give spurious readings and pose a laser-safety hazard.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a comma-delimited PNEZD coordinate file, the fields in order are:
options: Point, Northing, Easting, Z (elevation), Description
Prism, North, East, Zenith, Distance
Point, Easting, Northing, Zenith, Date
Position, Name, Elevation, Zone, Datum
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Point, Northing, Easting, Z (elevation), Description
quiz_questions.explanation: PNEZD = Point number, Northing, Easting, Z (elevation), Description. Confirming this order (vs. PENZD) on import prevents silently swapping northing and easting.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which file is most appropriate when the office needs to re-reduce a survey after discovering a wrong backsight coordinate?
options: The DWG drawing file
The PNEZD coordinate file
The raw data file (e.g., .rw5)
A PDF of the field notes
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The raw data file (e.g., .rw5)
quiz_questions.explanation: The raw file holds the observations (setups, backsights, HI/HR, angles, distances), so it can be reprocessed with the corrected backsight and all dependent points recomputed. A coordinate file cannot be re-reduced.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does resection (free-stationing) allow a surveyor to do?
options: Measure distance without a prism
Solve the instrument's own coordinates and orientation by sighting two or more known points, without occupying a monument
Eliminate the need for any control points
Read angles without face-left/face-right observations
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Solve the instrument's own coordinates and orientation by sighting two or more known points, without occupying a monument
quiz_questions.explanation: Resection sets up at an unknown point and observes known control to compute the station's coordinates and orientation, letting the crew set up wherever geometry and visibility are best.

quiz_questions.question_text: A resection is strongest (best conditioned) when the known control points sighted are:
options: Nearly in a straight line with the instrument
Clustered within a narrow angle
Well distributed with good intersection angles, ideally three or more for redundancy
All at the same distance and direction
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Well distributed with good intersection angles, ideally three or more for redundancy
quiz_questions.explanation: Well-distributed points with strong intersection angles give a robust solution, and three or more provide a residual check. Collinear or narrowly clustered points produce a weak, ill-conditioned result.

quiz_questions.question_text: In radial topography, a point is located from an oriented setup by:
options: Two distances from two stations
A single horizontal direction and slope distance, radiated to a coordinate
Differential leveling only
A GPS observation at each point
quiz_questions.correct_answer: A single horizontal direction and slope distance, radiated to a coordinate
quiz_questions.explanation: Radial topo locates each feature with one angle-and-distance shot; the processor radiates a coordinate as N = N₁ + D·cos(Az), E = E₁ + D·sin(Az). This is the everyday total-station method.

problem_templates.name: Radial coordinate from a setup (forward computation)
problem_templates.category: Total Stations and Electronic Surveying — Coordinate Geometry
problem_templates.question_template: From an oriented total-station setup at point A (N = {{n1}}.00 ft, E = {{e1}}.00 ft), a radial shot to point P has a horizontal distance of {{dist}}.00 ft at an azimuth of {{az}}°. What is the NORTHING of point P (in feet)?
problem_templates.explanation_template: The forward (radial) computation gives Nₚ = N₁ + D·cos(Az) = {{n1}}.00 + {{dist}}·cos({{az}}°) = {{n2:f2}} ft.

images_needed.caption: Setup A, azimuth Az and distance D to point P, with ΔN and ΔE components

problem_templates.name: Horizontal distance from slope distance and zenith angle
problem_templates.category: Total Stations and Electronic Surveying — Distance Reduction
problem_templates.question_template: A total station measures a slope distance of {{slope}}.00 ft to a prism at a zenith angle of {{zenith}}°00'. What is the HORIZONTAL distance (in feet)?
problem_templates.explanation_template: Reducing slope to horizontal: H = S·sin(Z) = {{slope}}.00 × sin({{zenith}}°) = {{horiz:f2}} ft. (Zenith is measured from vertical, so a level sight is 90°.)

images_needed.caption: Slope distance S at zenith angle Z resolving into horizontal H

problem_templates.name: Trigonometric elevation of a point from a setup
problem_templates.category: Total Stations and Electronic Surveying — Trigonometric Leveling
problem_templates.question_template: An instrument is set up at a point of elevation {{elevA}}.00 ft with a height of instrument HI = {{hi}}.0 ft (in tenths). It observes a prism (HR = {{hr}}.0 ft) at slope distance {{slope}}.00 ft and zenith angle {{zenith}}°00'. What is the ELEVATION of the observed point (in feet)? Use V = HI + S·cos(Z) − HR added to the setup elevation.
problem_templates.explanation_template: Trigonometric elevation: Elev = Elevₐ + HI + S·cos(Z) − HR = {{elevA}}.00 + {{hi}}.0 + {{slope}}·cos({{zenith}}°) − {{hr}}.0 = {{elevB:f2}} ft. When Z > 90° the cosine is negative (sight downhill).

images_needed.caption: Setup elevation, HI, slope distance at zenith Z, and HR to a prism, with the vertical component V
